learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of multiplayer games from local arcade cabinets through modern persistent online worlds
Classify multiplayer game genres by their synchronization requirements, player counts, and interaction models
Analyze how technical constraints historically shaped multiplayer game design decisions
Identify the key milestones that expanded what was possible in multiplayer game design
readings: Multiplayer Game Programming by Joshua Glazer and Sanjay Madhav, Ch. 1-2
Raph Koster, 'A Brief History of MMO Design'
GDC Vault: 'Thirty Years of Multiplayer' retrospective panel

lecture_notes.introduction: Multiplayer game design sits at the intersection of game design, distributed systems engineering, and social psychology. Every multiplayer game is fundamentally a piece of software that must maintain a shared reality across multiple machines connected by an unreliable, high-latency network, while simultaneously creating the illusion that all players exist in the same space at the same time. This course covers the design and technical challenges of building multiplayer experiences, from the networking foundations through social systems, competitive balance, and live operations. We begin with history because understanding how multiplayer games evolved reveals why certain design patterns exist. The constraints of 1990s dial-up modems shaped design decisions that persist in modern games. DOOM's peer-to-peer networking, Quake's client-server model, Ultima Online's persistent world architecture, and World of Warcraft's instance-based design each solved problems that the previous generation could not, and each introduced new problems that subsequent generations had to address.
key_concepts: Local multiplayer era (1970s-1980s): Pong, arcade cabinets, and split-screen console games where all players shared a single machine and single screen
LAN multiplayer era (1990s): DOOM, Quake, StarCraft, and the rise of competitive multiplayer on local area networks with sub-10ms latency
Early internet multiplayer: dial-up constraints (200-500ms latency, 56kbps bandwidth) forced fundamental design decisions about what information to synchronize
MMO era (1997-2010): Ultima Online, EverQuest, and World of Warcraft introduced persistent worlds with thousands of simultaneous players and new social dynamics
Modern multiplayer paradigms: battle royale (100 players, single instance), games-as-service (persistent progression), asymmetric multiplayer, and cooperative PvE
Synchronization model taxonomy: lockstep (RTS), client-server authoritative (FPS), rollback netcode (fighting games), and eventual consistency (MMOs)
Player interaction models: competitive (PvP), cooperative (PvE), asymmetric, parasocial (shared world, minimal direct interaction), and social/creative
The relationship between player count and design complexity: adding players increases technical, balance, and social complexity exponentially, not linearly
Platform-specific multiplayer considerations: console online services (PSN, Xbox Live, Nintendo Switch Online), PC (Steam, Epic), and mobile
The business model evolution: boxed product to subscription to free-to-play with cosmetic monetization, and how each model shapes multiplayer design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Historical comparison: we play or watch recordings of multiplayer sessions from five eras: a 1993 DOOM deathmatch (peer-to-peer, 4 players, LAN), a 2001 Counter-Strike match (client-server, 10 players, internet), a 2008 World of Warcraft raid (MMO instance, 25 players, persistent world), a 2018 Fortnite match (battle royale, 100 players, client-server), and a 2024 Helldivers 2 session (cooperative PvE, 4 players, dedicated servers). For each, we analyze the network model, player count, synchronization approach, and how technical constraints shaped the gameplay experience.
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2+ with Netcode for GameObjects package installed
Wireshark for network packet analysis
Local network test environment or virtual network adapter
Game recordings and postmortem documents from each era

review.value: DOOM used peer-to-peer because servers were expensive. Quake used client-server because cheating was rampant in P2P. Every multiplayer architecture decision is a response to a previous generation's limitations.

Lockstep: all clients simulate identically from shared inputs (RTS). Client-server authoritative: server owns truth (FPS). Rollback: predict locally, correct retroactively (fighting games). Each fits different latency tolerances.
A 2-player game has 1 interaction pair. A 4-player game has 6 pairs. A 100-player game has 4,950 pairs. Network bandwidth, server CPU, and design complexity scale with the square of player count.

Session-based games (matches) are stateless between sessions and easier to scale. Persistent worlds maintain state continuously and require database-backed architecture and careful conflict resolution.
Subscription MMOs incentivize time sinks. F2P games incentivize engagement loops and cosmetic appeal. The monetization model directly influences which multiplayer features get built.

learning_objectives: Compare client-server, peer-to-peer, and hybrid network architectures with their trade-offs
Evaluate which architecture suits specific game genres based on player count, latency sensitivity, and cheat prevention requirements
Implement a basic client-server connection using Unity Netcode for GameObjects
Analyze network traffic patterns using Wireshark to understand packet flow in multiplayer games
readings: Glazer and Madhav, Ch. 3-4: Network Topologies and Transport Protocols
Gaffer on Games: 'What Every Programmer Needs to Know About Game Networking'
Valve Developer Community: Source Multiplayer Networking documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: The network architecture of a multiplayer game is the foundation upon which every other technical and design decision rests. Choosing the wrong architecture is not a fixable mistake; it is a project-ending one that requires a rewrite of the entire networking layer. Client-server and peer-to-peer are the two fundamental approaches, with numerous hybrid variations. Each comes with inherent trade-offs in latency, bandwidth, cheat prevention, server cost, and scalability. This session provides a deep technical dive into each architecture, examining not just how they work but why you would choose one over the other for a specific game. We will also cover the transport layer decisions (TCP vs. UDP vs. reliable UDP) that affect every packet your game sends and receives. The practical component has you implementing a basic client-server connection and observing the actual network traffic in Wireshark, demystifying what happens on the wire when two game clients communicate.
key_concepts: Client-server authoritative model: a central server owns the canonical game state, clients send inputs and receive state updates, the server validates all actions to prevent cheating

Dedicated servers vs. listen servers: dedicated servers run on cloud infrastructure (higher cost, consistent performance), listen servers run on a player's machine (free, but host has latency advantage and hosting ends when they leave)
Peer-to-peer mesh topology: every client communicates with every other client, bandwidth scales as O(n^2) with player count, no single point of failure but difficult to prevent cheating
Peer-to-peer with relay: a relay server forwards packets between peers without processing game logic, reduces NAT traversal issues while keeping computation distributed
Hybrid architectures: peer-to-peer for latency-sensitive gameplay with a server for authoritative state validation (used in some fighting games and racing games)

Transport layer: TCP guarantees delivery order but adds latency from retransmission; UDP is faster but packets can arrive out of order or not at all; reliable UDP (ENet, LiteNetLib) provides a middle ground
NAT traversal: the majority of consumer internet connections use NAT, which complicates direct peer-to-peer connections. STUN, TURN, and ICE protocols facilitate hole-punching through NAT
Bandwidth budgeting: a typical multiplayer game allocates 64-128 kbps per player. Exceeding this budget causes packet loss on constrained connections

Architecture selection criteria: player count (P2P fails above 8-16), cheat sensitivity (competitive games need authoritative servers), latency budget (fighting games need sub-80ms), and cost constraints
Cloud server platforms: AWS GameLift, Google Cloud Game Servers, PlayFab, and Photon as managed multiplayer infrastructure options
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a basic client-server connection in Unity using Netcode for GameObjects. We create a simple scene where two clients connect to a host, spawn player objects, and synchronize position. While the game runs, we capture packets in Wireshark and analyze the traffic: packet size, frequency, protocol, and the overhead of reliable vs. unreliable messages. Students see the direct relationship between their code choices and the bytes traveling across the network.
setup_requirements: Unity project from Week 1 with Netcode for GameObjects
Wireshark installed and configured for local network capture
Two instances of the Unity editor or a build for second client
Network simulation tool (Clumsy or NetEm) for latency injection

review.value: The server is the source of truth. Clients propose actions; the server validates and applies them. This prevents cheating because no client can unilaterally modify game state.
TCP guarantees delivery and order but adds latency. UDP is faster but unreliable. Games use UDP for position updates (stale data is worse than lost data) and reliable UDP for important events.
Most home connections use NAT, blocking incoming connections. STUN discovers public IP/port, NAT hole-punching creates direct connections, TURN relays traffic when direct connection fails.
64-128 kbps per player is a practical budget. A 16-player game on a dedicated server needs 1-2 Mbps upload bandwidth. Exceeding per-player budgets causes packet loss and degraded experience.
Competitive + many players = dedicated server. Cooperative + few players = listen server or P2P. Fighting games = P2P with rollback. MMO = dedicated servers with instancing.

learning_objectives: Implement an authoritative server that validates client inputs and maintains canonical game state
Design server tick rates and client update frequencies appropriate for different game genres
Handle client connections, disconnections, and reconnections gracefully
Implement server-side hit detection and action validation
readings: Glazer and Madhav, Ch. 5-6: Client-Server Architecture
Gabriel Gambetta, 'Fast-Paced Multiplayer' series (Parts 1-2)
Overwatch GDC Talk: 'Overwatch Gameplay Architecture and Netcode'

lecture_notes.introduction: The client-server model is the dominant architecture for competitive multiplayer games because it solves the fundamental trust problem: in a peer-to-peer game, every client must trust every other client to be honest about the game state. In a client-server game, only the server must be trusted. The server validates every action, detects impossible states, and broadcasts the authoritative version of reality to all clients. But building a robust client-server game is substantially more complex than building a single-player game. The server must process inputs from all connected clients, simulate the game world, validate actions, resolve conflicts, and broadcast state updates, all within a fixed time budget determined by the tick rate. This session dives deep into server architecture: how to structure the game loop for multiplayer, how to choose tick rates, how to validate client inputs without creating unacceptable latency, and how to handle the messy realities of clients connecting, disconnecting, and reconnecting mid-game.
key_concepts: Server tick rate: how often the server updates game state (20 Hz for MMOs, 64 Hz for competitive FPS, 128 Hz for Counter-Strike tournament servers), and the latency implications of each choice
Client update rate: how often the client sends input to the server and receives state updates, which can differ from the server tick rate to manage bandwidth
Server game loop: receive inputs from all clients, simulate physics and game logic, validate actions, resolve conflicts, broadcast state updates, all within one tick period
Input validation: the server must verify that every client action is legal (player cannot move faster than max speed, cannot fire faster than weapon rate, cannot hit targets behind walls)
Server-side hit detection: the server rolls back time to the shooting client's perspective, checks if the shot would have hit, and resolves using the server's authoritative state
Connection management: tracking client sessions, handling graceful disconnects (player quit) vs. ungraceful disconnects (network failure), and implementing reconnection with state restoration
Server authority boundaries: the server is authoritative over game state but may delegate visual effects, camera, and UI to the client for responsiveness
Determinism vs. authority: deterministic simulation (same inputs produce same outputs) simplifies server validation but requires careful handling of floating-point differences across platforms
Server resource budgeting: CPU time per tick, memory per connected client, and bandwidth per update constrain what the server can simulate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Extend the Unity Netcode project to implement server-authoritative movement: the client sends movement inputs (WASD), the server validates speed and applies movement, then broadcasts the authoritative position to all clients. We then add a simple projectile system with server-side hit detection. Students observe the behavioral difference between client-predicted and server-authoritative movement by introducing 200ms of artificial latency.
setup_requirements: Unity Netcode project from Week 2
Network latency simulation tool configured for 200ms round-trip
Profiling tools for measuring server tick time
Second Unity editor instance or standalone build

review.value: Higher tick rate means smoother gameplay and more responsive hit detection, but costs more CPU and bandwidth. 64 Hz is standard for competitive FPS, 20-30 Hz for slower-paced games.

Never trust client data. Validate every input against server state: position, velocity, action timing, and resource availability. If the client says it fired a bullet, verify ammo count on the server.
When a client reconnects, the server must send a full state snapshot rather than accumulated deltas. Reserve a slot for the disconnected player for 30-60 seconds before freeing it.
The server rewinds time by the shooting client's latency, checks the shot against historical positions, and resolves the hit. This ensures fairness regardless of ping differences between players.

learning_objectives: Implement peer-to-peer networking with input synchronization for a two-player game
Explain rollback netcode and its advantages for latency-sensitive genres like fighting games
Compare delay-based and rollback-based input handling with their impact on player experience
Analyze the GGPO library's approach to rollback networking
readings: Infil, 'Explaining Rollback Netcode' (comprehensive guide)
GGPO library documentation and source code overview
GDC Vault: 'It's All About the Ticks: Rollback Networking in Mortal Kombat and Injustice 2'

lecture_notes.introduction: While client-server dominates competitive multiplayer, peer-to-peer remains the optimal architecture for certain genres, particularly fighting games and small-session cooperative games. The reason is latency. In a client-server model, every input travels from Client A to the Server to Client B, adding at least one extra network hop. In P2P, inputs travel directly from Client A to Client B, reducing round-trip time by roughly half. For fighting games where a single frame (16.7ms at 60fps) can determine the outcome of an exchange, this latency reduction is the difference between playable and unplayable. Rollback netcode, pioneered by the GGPO library, takes P2P networking further by eliminating the perceived latency entirely for the local player. Instead of waiting for the remote player's input before advancing the game, the local client predicts what the remote player will do, simulates forward, and then corrects retroactively if the prediction was wrong. When the prediction is right, which is most of the time for human inputs, the game feels perfectly responsive. When the prediction is wrong, the game 'rolls back' to the last confirmed state and re-simulates, creating brief visual corrections that are usually imperceptible.
key_concepts: Delay-based netcode: both clients wait for all inputs to arrive before advancing each frame. Adds consistent input delay equal to one-way network latency. Feels sluggish at high ping.
Rollback netcode: the local client runs ahead using predicted inputs, then corrects retroactively if predictions were wrong. Local play feels zero-latency. Mispredictions cause brief visual pops.
Deterministic simulation requirement: rollback only works if the game is fully deterministic (same inputs always produce same state). Floating-point determinism and fixed-point math become critical.
State serialization and restoration: to roll back, the game must be able to save and restore complete game state rapidly. This constrains how state is structured and what data types are used.
Input prediction strategies: most human inputs are 'continue doing what I was doing.' Predicting that the remote player's input stays the same as the previous frame is correct 85-95% of the time.
Rollback window: the number of frames the game runs ahead of confirmed inputs. Typically 2-7 frames. Larger windows tolerate more latency but increase the visual impact of mispredictions.
GGPO (Good Game, Peace Out): the open-source library that popularized rollback netcode. Provides a framework for deterministic simulation, state save/load, input prediction, and rollback.
Visual correction techniques: interpolation and blending during corrections to minimize the perceptual impact of mispredicted frames
P2P limitations: NAT traversal complexity, difficulty scaling beyond 2-4 players, and vulnerability to cheating without a trusted authority
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side comparison: we run two fighting game prototypes, one with delay-based netcode and one with rollback, over a simulated 100ms connection. Students play both and experience the feel difference firsthand. We then examine the GGPO integration code, tracing how input prediction, state save, and rollback correction work in practice. Students implement basic input prediction in the Unity project.
setup_requirements: Fighting game prototype with both netcode modes (provided on course portal)
GGPO library documentation and sample code
Network simulation at 50ms, 100ms, and 200ms one-way latency
Frame-by-frame recording tool to visualize rollback corrections

review.value: Delay-based: every input feels sluggish proportional to ping. Rollback: local inputs feel instant, occasional visual pops on misprediction. Players overwhelmingly prefer rollback.
Rollback demands bit-perfect determinism. The same inputs must produce the exact same state on both machines. Floating-point variance between platforms breaks this. Use fixed-point math.
Predicting 'same input as last frame' is correct 85-95% of the time for fighting games. This means rollback corrections are rare and brief, maintaining the illusion of zero-latency play.
Rollback saves state every frame. State serialization must be fast (under 1ms) and compact. Structure game state for rapid copying: contiguous memory, minimal pointers, value types over references.

learning_objectives: Implement client-side prediction for responsive movement in a client-server game
Apply server reconciliation to correct prediction errors without visible teleportation
Implement entity interpolation for smooth rendering of other players' movements
Analyze the trade-offs between responsiveness and accuracy in latency compensation
readings: Gabriel Gambetta, 'Fast-Paced Multiplayer' series (Parts 3-4)
Gaffer on Games: 'Snapshot Interpolation' and 'State Synchronization'
Rocket League GDC Talk: 'It IS Rocket Science: Physics Networking in Rocket League'

lecture_notes.introduction: Network latency is the fundamental enemy of multiplayer game feel. A player on a 100ms round-trip connection who presses a movement key must wait 100ms for the server to process that input and send back the result. At 60fps, that is six frames of input delay, which makes the game feel unresponsive and disconnected. Latency compensation techniques solve this problem by letting the client predict the outcome of its own actions immediately while waiting for server confirmation. Client-side prediction simulates the player's movement locally based on their inputs, giving instant visual feedback. Server reconciliation corrects the client's prediction when the server's authoritative result arrives, ideally without the player noticing. Entity interpolation ensures that other players appear to move smoothly despite receiving position updates only 20-64 times per second. Together, these techniques create the illusion of a responsive, smooth multiplayer experience even over connections with significant latency. This session covers the implementation of each technique and the subtle trade-offs involved.
key_concepts: Client-side prediction: the client simulates its own movement immediately using local inputs, then applies the server's authoritative correction when it arrives. The player sees responsive movement at all times.

Server reconciliation: when the server's authoritative state arrives, the client compares it to its predicted state. If they differ, the client replays all unacknowledged inputs from the server's state forward to produce a corrected prediction.

Input buffering: the client stores a buffer of inputs sent to the server. When the server acknowledges an input, the client discards all inputs up to that point and replays the remaining ones against the server state.

Entity interpolation: remote players are rendered at a position in the past (typically 100ms), interpolating between two received snapshots. This ensures smooth visual movement despite discrete updates.

Extrapolation vs. interpolation: interpolation is smooth but adds visual latency. Extrapolation is responsive but can create overshooting when players change direction. Interpolation is preferred for most games.

Lag compensation for hit detection: the server stores a history of player positions and 'rewinds time' when processing a shot, checking if the target was at the predicted position from the shooter's perspective.
The feeling of being shot behind cover: a consequence of lag compensation. The shooter saw you in the open from their delayed perspective. This is correct behavior but feels unfair to the target.
Jitter and packet loss handling: network conditions fluctuate. Adaptive interpolation buffers expand during jitter and contract during stable conditions to maintain smooth rendering.

Physics prediction challenges: predicting physics interactions (collisions, projectiles affected by gravity) is more complex than predicting simple movement. Rocket League's approach to physics networking.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full implementation of the latency compensation stack in Unity: client-side prediction with server reconciliation for the local player, entity interpolation for remote players, and lag-compensated hit detection for a simple shooting mechanic. We test with 0ms, 100ms, and 300ms simulated latency to demonstrate how each technique contributes to perceived responsiveness.
setup_requirements: Unity Netcode project from previous weeks
Network latency simulation at multiple levels (0ms, 100ms, 300ms)
Debug visualization for predicted vs. server positions
Frame recording for analyzing interpolation smoothness

review.value: Client predicts locally for responsiveness. Server corrects authoritatively for accuracy. Reconciliation replays buffered inputs against server state to smoothly bridge the gap.
Remote players are rendered 100ms in the past, smoothly interpolating between snapshots. This adds visual latency but eliminates jitter. The trade-off is universally accepted.

Lag compensation means a shooter sees and hits you based on where you were 100-200ms ago. The server validates this as correct. The solution is to accept this trade-off or reduce maximum allowed ping.

The interpolation buffer should expand during network jitter (more buffered snapshots) and contract during stable conditions (less visual latency). Static buffers cause either jitter or unnecessary delay.

learning_objectives: Implement efficient state synchronization using delta compression and prioritized updates
Design interest management systems that reduce bandwidth by filtering irrelevant state updates
Apply data compression techniques specific to game networking: quantization, bit packing, and Huffman coding
Measure and optimize bandwidth usage to stay within per-player budgets
readings: Glazer and Madhav, Ch. 7-8: State Synchronization and Optimization
Gaffer on Games: 'Serialization Strategies'
Epic Games: Unreal Engine Replication documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: A multiplayer game with 64 players, each with position (3 floats), rotation (4 floats), velocity (3 floats), animation state, health, and equipment data, generates a staggering amount of data every server tick. At 64 Hz with 64 players, the naive approach of sending every player's full state every tick would consume over 10 Mbps of server bandwidth, far exceeding practical limits. State synchronization optimization is the art of sending the minimum data necessary to maintain a coherent shared reality. This session covers the core optimization techniques: delta compression (send only what changed), interest management (send only what matters to each client), quantization (reduce precision to save bits), bit packing (eliminate wasted space in data serialization), and prioritized updates (send important changes more frequently). Together, these techniques can reduce bandwidth by 90-95% compared to naive synchronization, making large-scale multiplayer games technically and economically feasible.
key_concepts: Delta compression: instead of sending full state each tick, track what changed since the last acknowledged snapshot and send only the deltas. Reduces bandwidth by 80-95% for typical game states.

Interest management: each client only receives updates about entities within a relevant radius, visible area, or logical group. A player does not need state updates about enemies on the other side of the map.
Area of interest (AOI) systems: spatial partitioning (grid-based, quadtree) determines which entities are relevant to each client. AOI boundaries must be larger than visual range to prevent pop-in.
Quantization: reducing floating-point precision to save bandwidth. Positions can be quantized to millimeter precision (saving 50% vs. full float), rotations to 10-bit angles (saving 66%).
Bit packing: writing values using exactly the number of bits needed rather than rounding up to byte boundaries. A health value of 0-100 needs 7 bits, not 32.
Huffman coding and variable-length encoding: frequently occurring values (common animation states, zero deltas) use fewer bits than rare values, further reducing average message size.

Prioritized updates: not all state changes are equally important. Position changes for nearby visible players get higher priority than distant players. Priority-based scheduling ensures the most important updates fit within bandwidth budgets.
Reliable vs. unreliable synchronization: position updates use unreliable delivery (stale data is useless), while game events (kills, pickups, score changes) use reliable delivery (must not be lost).
Object relevancy: combining interest management with ownership and visibility flags to determine exactly which state updates each client receives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bandwidth optimization lab: starting with a naive multiplayer implementation that sends full state every tick, students apply delta compression, quantization, bit packing, and interest management one technique at a time, measuring bandwidth reduction after each step. By the end, the same game uses 90% less bandwidth. Wireshark captures verify the actual data reduction on the wire.
setup_requirements: Unity Netcode project with 16+ simulated entities
Wireshark for bandwidth measurement before and after optimization
Custom bandwidth monitoring overlay in the Unity project
Profiling tools for measuring serialization CPU cost

review.value: In a typical game, only 5-20% of state changes each tick. Sending only deltas reduces bandwidth by 80-95%. Track acknowledged state per client to compute accurate deltas.
A player in a 100-player game only needs updates about the 10-20 nearest players. Spatial partitioning filters irrelevant updates, reducing per-client bandwidth proportionally.
Position: 0.01 unit precision is sufficient for most games (3 bytes per axis vs. 4). Rotation: 10-bit angles provide sub-degree precision. Health: 7 bits for 0-100 range.

When bandwidth is constrained, send the most important updates first: nearby visible players > nearby audible players > distant relevant players. Update frequency decreases with distance and relevance.

learning_objectives: Design matchmaking systems that balance match quality, queue time, and player pool constraints
Implement ELO, Glicko-2, and TrueSkill rating systems and understand their mathematical foundations
Handle edge cases in matchmaking: new players, parties, regional preferences, and low-population scenarios
Evaluate matchmaking system performance using match quality metrics
readings: Microsoft Research: 'TrueSkill: A Bayesian Skill Rating System'
Riot Games Engineering Blog: 'Under the Hood of League of Legends Matchmaking'
Josh Menke (Blizzard), 'Skill Rating Systems' GDC Talk

lecture_notes.introduction: Matchmaking is the invisible system that makes or breaks competitive multiplayer games. When matchmaking works well, players experience fairly matched games that feel challenging but winnable, and they keep playing. When matchmaking fails, players experience one-sided stomps or impossibly difficult opponents, and they quit. The core challenge of matchmaking is a multi-objective optimization problem: minimize queue time, maximize match quality (closeness of skill between teams), respect geographic preferences (minimize latency), accommodate parties of varying sizes, and do all of this with whatever player pool happens to be online at this moment. These objectives conflict: the best skill match might require longer queue times or higher ping. This session covers the mathematical foundations of skill rating systems, the engineering of matchmaking queues, and the design decisions that determine whether players perceive the system as fair.
key_concepts: ELO rating system: the original skill rating from chess. A single number representing skill, adjusted after each match based on outcome relative to expected outcome. Simple but limited for team games.
Glicko-2 system: extends ELO with a rating deviation (uncertainty) that increases when a player is inactive and decreases with more games. More accurately represents skill when data is limited.
TrueSkill (Microsoft): Bayesian skill rating designed for multiplayer team games. Maintains a skill mean and uncertainty for each player. Handles team games, free-for-all, and parties.
Matchmaking queue design: players enter a queue, the system periodically evaluates possible matches, and selects the best available match. Queue timeout increases search radius over time.
Match quality metrics: expected match closeness (probability that either team wins should be near 50%), ping variance between players, party size balance, and time spent in queue
New player handling (calibration): new players need 10-20 games to reach an accurate rating. During calibration, match them against diverse skill levels and use larger rating adjustments per game.

Party matchmaking: groups of friends queuing together complicate skill matching. A party of one Diamond and one Bronze player should not be matched against two Gold players. Weight toward the highest-skilled member.
Regional matchmaking and server selection: prefer low-ping servers, but expand to higher-ping regions if the local player pool is insufficient for a quality match
Low-population solutions: wider skill matching, cross-platform pools, AI backfill, and reducing queue requirements during off-peak hours

Smurfing and boosting: skill rating manipulation where high-skill players create new accounts or carry low-skill friends. Detection through performance metrics, hardware fingerprinting, and behavioral analysis.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Matchmaking simulation: using a Python simulation, we create a pool of 1,000 players with normally distributed skill ratings and simulate a matchmaking system. Students adjust parameters (skill range tolerance, queue timeout, party handling rules) and observe the impact on match quality, queue times, and player satisfaction metrics. We compare ELO, Glicko-2, and TrueSkill convergence rates on the same dataset.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with numpy and matplotlib for simulation
Matchmaking simulation framework (provided on course portal)
Spreadsheet for match quality metric analysis
Rating system implementation libraries for comparison

review.value: ELO: simple, single number, proven for 1v1. TrueSkill: handles teams, parties, and uncertainty. Use ELO for 1v1 games, TrueSkill or Glicko-2 for team-based games.
Tighter skill matching means longer queues. The system must expand the acceptable skill range as queue time increases, with player-facing messaging about expected wait times.
New players need 10-20 games for accurate placement. During calibration, larger rating adjustments per game enable faster convergence. Hide the rating number during calibration to reduce anxiety.

A new account performing at a level 2+ standard deviations above average for new players is likely a smurf. Accelerate their rating adjustment to place them quickly, reducing the number of unfair matches.

learning_objectives: Classify common cheat types in multiplayer games and their technical mechanisms
Design server-side validation systems that prevent the most impactful cheats
Evaluate client-side anti-cheat solutions (EAC, BattlEye, Vanguard) and their trade-offs
Develop a comprehensive anti-cheat strategy combining technical, design, and community approaches
readings: Matt Pritchard, 'How to Hurt the Hackers: The Scoop on Internet Cheating and How You Can Combat It'
GDC Vault: 'Anti-Cheat for Multiplayer Games' by Valve
Riot Games Vanguard technical blog posts on kernel-level anti-cheat

lecture_notes.introduction: Cheating is an existential threat to competitive multiplayer games. A single cheater in a match of legitimate players ruins the experience for everyone, and the perception that a game has a cheating problem can drive away the player base faster than any other issue. Anti-cheat is not a single system but a multi-layered strategy combining server-side validation, client-side detection, behavioral analysis, and community reporting. No single approach is sufficient because cheaters are adversaries who actively work to circumvent every defense. This session examines the full spectrum of cheating in multiplayer games, from simple exploits to sophisticated memory hacking, and the defenses available for each. We will analyze the technical mechanisms behind common cheats (aimbots, wallhacks, speed hacks, teleportation), the detection methods used by commercial anti-cheat solutions, and the controversial trade-offs involved in kernel-level anti-cheat systems. The design perspective is equally important: some game designs are inherently more resistant to cheating than others, and thoughtful design choices can reduce the impact and motivation for cheating.
key_concepts: Cheat taxonomy: memory hacking (aimbots, wallhacks), network manipulation (lag switches, packet injection), automation (bots, macros), exploit abuse (duplication glitches, collision bypasses), and social engineering

Server-side validation as the first line of defense: the server should validate all client actions against game rules. If movement speed, fire rate, or damage values are server-authoritative, many cheats become impossible.
The limits of server authority: the server cannot prevent information-based cheats (wallhacks) that exploit data already sent to the client for rendering purposes

Client-side anti-cheat: software running on the player's machine that detects known cheat signatures, memory modifications, and suspicious process behavior. Examples: Easy Anti-Cheat, BattlEye, Vanguard.
Kernel-level anti-cheat (ring 0): provides deeper system access for more effective detection but raises serious privacy and security concerns. Riot's Vanguard runs at boot as a driver.
Statistical detection: machine learning models that identify statistically impossible performance (inhuman reaction times, impossible accuracy percentages) without inspecting the client machine
Behavioral analysis: detecting patterns that distinguish cheaters from skilled players: consistent inhuman micro-adjustments, perfect tracking through walls, and reaction times below human minimums
Information restriction: designing networking to minimize the data sent to each client. If the client never receives enemy positions behind walls, wallhacks cannot reveal them.
The arms race: anti-cheat and cheat developers engage in a continuous escalation. Each new defense is eventually circumvented, requiring ongoing investment in anti-cheat development.
Community reporting and trust systems: player reports, replay review systems, and trust scores that match suspected cheaters with other suspected cheaters (trust-based matchmaking)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Anti-cheat analysis lab: using a simple multiplayer demo with deliberately vulnerable networking (client-authoritative movement, full state broadcast), students create and detect simple cheats: speed modification, teleportation, and information extraction. They then implement server-side validations that prevent each cheat. This exercise demonstrates why server authority is essential and where it falls short.
setup_requirements: Deliberately vulnerable multiplayer demo (provided on course portal)
Cheat Engine (educational use) for memory inspection
Server-side validation framework for implementing fixes
Statistical analysis tools for behavioral detection examples

review.value: Server-side validation prevents the most impactful cheats (speed, damage, teleportation) with zero impact on legitimate players. Always start here before adding client-side measures.

If the client never receives enemy positions behind walls, wallhacks are impossible. Send only what the client needs to render its current view. This is the strongest defense against information cheats.
Kernel-level access enables the most effective cheat detection but introduces security vulnerabilities, privacy concerns, and compatibility issues. The community is deeply divided on this trade-off.

Effective anti-cheat combines server validation (prevents exploits), client scanning (detects known cheats), statistical analysis (catches unknown cheats), and community reporting (catches what automation misses).

class_schedule.topic: Social Features: Friends, Guilds, and Communication
learning_objectives: Design social systems that encourage healthy player interactions and long-term retention
Implement friends lists, party systems, and guild structures with appropriate persistence
Create communication systems (text chat, voice chat, ping systems) that balance expressiveness with safety
Apply social graph analysis to understand and leverage player relationship networks
readings: Amy Jo Kim, 'Community Building on the Web', Ch. 4-6
Raph Koster, 'Social Architectures in MMOs'
GDC Vault: 'The Science Behind Social Systems in Games'

lecture_notes.introduction: The social systems in a multiplayer game are often more important to long-term retention than the gameplay itself. Players return to games not because the core loop is compelling, but because their friends are online. A player with three or more friends in a game is significantly less likely to churn than a solo player, regardless of game quality. This session covers the design and implementation of social features: friends lists, parties, clans and guilds, chat systems, and the broader social architecture that shapes how players interact. We will examine how games create social bonds between strangers, how communication tools affect player behavior, and how social graph data can inform game design and retention strategy. The key insight is that social systems must be designed intentionally, not added as an afterthought. The structure of social features, the friction of forming groups, the visibility of player activity, and the persistence of social connections all shape the community that emerges.
key_concepts: Social graph theory: players as nodes, relationships as edges. Analyzing connection density, cluster formation, and network health as predictors of game longevity.
Friends system design: adding, removing, blocking, and managing friend lists. Cross-platform friend systems (Epic, Steam, platform-agnostic). Friend activity feeds and presence indicators.
Party system: temporary groups for queueing and playing together. Party formation, invitation, leader authority, and persistence across game modes and sessions.
Guild/clan systems: persistent player organizations with hierarchy, roles, permissions, shared resources, and communication channels. Guilds create social obligation that drives retention.
Text chat architecture: client-server chat routing, channel design (global, team, party, guild, whisper), profanity filtering, and moderation tools
Voice chat integration: push-to-talk vs. open mic, spatial audio in-game, party voice channels, and voice moderation challenges including toxicity detection

Ping and contextual communication systems: Apex Legends' ping system as a case study in enabling strategic communication without voice chat, accessible to all players including those with language barriers or disabilities

Toxicity management: automated detection of harassment, muting and reporting systems, graduated punishment (warning, mute, suspension, ban), and the design challenge of balancing expression with safety
Social retention mechanics: daily login bonuses for parties, shared progression goals, guild challenges, and friend referral rewards that leverage social obligation
Privacy and safety: age-appropriate social features (COPPA compliance), parental controls, opt-in vs. opt-out communication, and protecting vulnerable players
lecture_notes.demonstration: Social system design exercise: students design the complete social architecture for a hypothetical cooperative PvE game. They spec the friends list, party system, guild system, and communication tools, including wireframes for the social UI, a data model for social relationships, and a moderation workflow. The class then evaluates each design for social friction points, toxicity vectors, and retention potential.
setup_requirements: Wireframing tool (Figma, Miro, or paper) for social UI design
Social system data model template (entity relationship diagram)
Examples of social UIs from existing multiplayer games
Chat moderation API documentation (Perspective API by Google)

review.value: Players with 3+ in-game friends have 2-3x higher retention than solo players. Every design decision should reduce friction for forming and maintaining friendships.
Apex Legends proved that a well-designed contextual ping system can replace voice chat for tactical communication, reducing toxicity while increasing accessibility.
Guilds create social obligation: players log in because their guildmates are depending on them. This is the strongest retention mechanic in multiplayer games and the core of MMO design.
Warning > 24-hour mute > 7-day suspension > permanent ban. Graduated systems reform more behavior than immediate bans while reserving permanent action for repeat offenders.

learning_objectives: Design cooperative mechanics that create genuine interdependence between players
Balance difficulty scaling for varying party sizes and skill levels
Create asymmetric roles that give each player a meaningful contribution to the team
Implement AI director systems that adapt to player performance in real time
readings: GDC Vault: Left 4 Dead's 'AI Director' postmortem by Valve
It Takes Two: GDC postmortem on cooperative design
Deep Rock Galactic developer diary on asymmetric class design

lecture_notes.introduction: Cooperative game design is fundamentally different from competitive game design. In competitive games, the challenge comes from other players. In cooperative games, the challenge comes from the game itself, and the players' relationship with each other is collaborative, not adversarial. This distinction has profound implications for every design decision. The best cooperative games create situations where players genuinely need each other, where no single player can succeed alone, and where the group's success feels like a shared achievement. This session examines the design principles that create compelling cooperative experiences: meaningful roles, complementary abilities, shared and individual objectives, difficulty scaling, and the AI systems that create dynamic challenge. We will study games that exemplify outstanding cooperative design, from Left 4 Dead's AI Director to Deep Rock Galactic's asymmetric classes to It Takes Two's paired mechanics, and extract the design patterns that make them work.
key_concepts: Interdependence design: creating situations where players cannot succeed alone. Locks that require two keys, enemies that require coordinated attacks, puzzles with components only visible to different players.

Asymmetric roles: giving each player a distinct set of abilities that fills a unique team function. The healer, the tank, the DPS model from MMOs is one approach; Deep Rock Galactic's class-specific traversal tools is another.

Complementary ability design: abilities that become more powerful when combined with another player's abilities. Overwatch's nano-boost + ultimate combos. Divinity: Original Sin's element combination system.
Difficulty scaling: adjusting enemy health, spawn rates, AI aggression, and puzzle complexity based on party size. The challenge should feel equivalent whether 2 or 4 players are present.

AI Director concept (Left 4 Dead): a system that monitors player performance (health, ammo, pacing, stress) and dynamically adjusts spawns, item placement, and event timing to create a dramatic rhythm.

Shared vs. individual objectives: cooperative games benefit from both group goals (defeat the boss) and individual sub-goals (collect personal resources, complete personal challenges) to prevent free-riding.

Communication requirements: cooperative games must decide how much communication is required for success. Casual co-op should be playable without voice chat; hardcore co-op can assume coordinated communication.

Carry prevention: ensuring one highly skilled player cannot trivialize content for the group. Mechanics that require participation from all members prevent the experience from becoming a spectator sport.

Revive and failure mechanics: how death and failure work in co-op shapes the emotional experience. Team wipes (shared failure) vs. individual downs (partial failure with recovery) create different dynamics.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cooperative design analysis: the class plays through two cooperative game segments: one with strong interdependence (all players are essential) and one where one player can carry the team. We analyze the experience afterward, identifying the specific design decisions that created or prevented meaningful cooperation. Students then design a cooperative encounter that requires contribution from all team members.
setup_requirements: Two cooperative game demos or segments (provided)
Design document template for cooperative encounters
Whiteboard for collaborative encounter design
Difficulty scaling calculation spreadsheet

review.value: When players genuinely need each other, success feels like a team achievement. When one player can solo the content, others feel like spectators. Design encounters that require multiple roles.
Monitor player performance in real time and adjust difficulty to maintain dramatic tension. Struggling teams get fewer enemies and more supplies. Dominant teams face escalating challenge.
Design encounters that require simultaneous action from multiple players: buttons pressed at the same time, threats from multiple directions, mechanics that demand role-specific abilities.

Design for the lowest common denominator of communication. If your game requires voice chat to be playable, you have excluded the majority of players. Ping systems and visual cues should be sufficient for core gameplay.

learning_objectives: Apply competitive balance principles to create fair matches between players of varying skill
Design ranked ladder systems with divisions, seasons, and promotion mechanics
Analyze player psychology around ranked play: ladder anxiety, rank distribution, and progression satisfaction
Implement anti-smurf and anti-boosting measures that protect ranked integrity
readings: David Sirlin, 'Playing to Win' chapters on game balance
Riot Games Engineering: 'The Math Behind League of Legends Rankings'
GDC Vault: 'Ranked Play Design: Lessons from Fighting Games and Beyond'

lecture_notes.introduction: Competitive balance is the foundation of fair multiplayer games. If one character, strategy, or weapon is clearly dominant, the game collapses into a single optimal playstyle that rewards knowledge of the meta over skill and creativity. Ranked systems add a visible progression layer on top of competitive play, giving players a sense of their skill level and a ladder to climb. But ranked system design is as much psychology as engineering. The rank distribution, the visibility of rank changes, the presence of loss protection, and the season reset mechanics all profoundly affect player motivation, anxiety, and retention. This session covers both the game balance side, how to create games where multiple strategies are viable and matches feel fair, and the ranked system side, how to design visible skill progression that motivates players to keep improving.
key_concepts: Balance philosophy: perfect balance (every option is equally viable) vs. dynamic balance (the meta shifts over time through patches). Most competitive games target dynamic balance with a healthy meta rotation.

Dominant strategy detection: using win rate data, pick rate data, and statistical significance to identify options that are too strong or too weak. A character with a 55%+ win rate and 30%+ pick rate is likely overpowered.

Counter-play design: every strong strategy should have a counter-strategy that is accessible and effective. If no counter exists, the strategy is dominant and must be nerfed or its counter must be buffed.
Ranked tier systems: Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond, Master, Grandmaster (or similar). Tiers provide identity and clear goals. Division points within tiers create micro-progression.

Rank distribution design: most games target a bell curve with the median at Gold/Silver. Placement matches seed players near their expected tier. The distribution should feel fair: most players are average.

Season resets: soft resets (compress toward the median) vs. hard resets (everyone starts from scratch). Soft resets maintain some progression while preventing stagnation. Hard resets create chaos at season start.

Ladder anxiety: the fear of playing ranked because losing feels worse than winning feels good (loss aversion). Mitigated through loss protection at tier boundaries, hidden MMR, and emphasizing improvement over rank.
Promotion and demotion mechanics: promotion series (must win 2/3 to advance) create milestone moments but also increase anxiety. Free demotion protection reduces fear of playing.
Smurf and boost detection: accounts performing significantly above their rank are flagged for accelerated ranking. Duo queues between highly disparate ranks are restricted to prevent boosting.
Seasonal rewards: exclusive cosmetics, titles, and borders for end-of-season rank achievement. Rewards incentivize ranked play and create prestige for high achievement.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ranked system simulation: using the matchmaking simulation from Week 7, we add a ranked tier system with divisions, promotion series, and seasonal resets. Students observe how different design choices (soft vs. hard reset, promotion series vs. automatic, loss protection) affect rank distribution, player progression speed, and the accuracy of rank as a skill predictor. We analyze the results statistically.
setup_requirements: Python matchmaking simulation extended with ranked tiers
Statistical analysis tools for rank distribution visualization
Spreadsheets for balance metric tracking (win rates, pick rates)
Game balance case studies with historical data

review.value: The best competitive games have a balance triangle: Strategy A beats B, B beats C, C beats A. This creates a healthy meta where the counter to the dominant strategy is always available.
Target a normal distribution: 5% Bronze, 15% Silver, 30% Gold, 25% Platinum, 15% Diamond, 8% Master, 2% Grandmaster. Adjust thresholds until the distribution matches design intent.

Loss protection at tier boundaries (cannot demote from Gold to Silver from a single loss), visible improvement metrics beyond rank (personal bests, improvement trends), and unranked modes as pressure relief.
2-3 month seasons provide enough time for players to reach their true rank while creating regular fresh-start excitement. Shorter seasons frustrate players who cannot invest enough time.

learning_objectives: Design live operations pipelines that deliver regular content updates on a sustainable cadence
Create battle pass and seasonal content systems that drive engagement and revenue
Implement feature flags and server-side configuration for live game management
Plan live events and limited-time modes that create urgency and community excitement
readings: Deconstructing Fortnite's Live Operations Model (industry analysis)
GDC Vault: 'Running a Live Game: Lessons from Destiny 2'
Epic Games: 'How Fortnite Runs Live Events at Scale'

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern multiplayer games are not shipped and forgotten; they are operated as ongoing services. Live operations, or LiveOps, is the discipline of continuously updating, balancing, and evolving a game after launch to maintain and grow its player base. The most successful multiplayer games of the past five years, including Fortnite, Apex Legends, Destiny 2, and Genshin Impact, all share a common trait: a rigorous live operations cadence that delivers fresh content on a predictable schedule. This session covers the design and operational systems that make live games possible: seasonal content structures, battle pass design, live events, feature flags, and the content pipeline that must deliver on a relentless schedule. We will examine the organizational and technical infrastructure required to sustain a live game, the metrics that guide live ops decisions, and the creative challenge of keeping a game fresh without breaking what makes it work.
key_concepts: Seasonal structure: 2-3 month seasons with a thematic identity, new content, meta changes, and progression reset. Seasons create natural re-engagement points and marketing moments.
Battle pass design: a tiered reward track with free and premium tiers. Players progress through gameplay, earning cosmetics, currency, and exclusive items. The pass must feel rewarding at both tiers.
Content cadence: weekly, bi-weekly, and seasonal content drops on a published schedule. Players should always know when new content is coming. Predictability builds habitual engagement.

Live events: time-limited, often one-time experiences that create shared community moments. Fortnite's in-game concerts and map-changing events set the standard. Events drive social media sharing and re-engagement.
Limited-time modes (LTMs): temporary game modes that add variety without permanently complicating the matchmaking pool. Rotate LTMs to maintain freshness and test new mechanics.
Feature flags: server-side toggles that enable or disable features without client updates. Essential for rolling out features gradually, A/B testing, and emergency kill switches.
Server-side configuration: game balance values, event schedules, and content availability controlled from the server, allowing changes without requiring a client patch.
Content pipeline for live games: pre-production of seasonal content 2-3 seasons ahead, with a production team, art pipeline, and QA process that delivers on schedule reliably.
Metrics-driven live ops: DAU/MAU ratio, session length trends, battle pass completion rates, and revenue per update guide content investment decisions
Burnout and crunch in live ops: the relentless content cadence creates significant pressure on development teams. Sustainable live ops requires advance planning and sufficient staffing.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Battle pass design workshop: students design a complete seasonal battle pass for a hypothetical multiplayer game. They create 100 tiers across free and premium tracks, define reward types and pacing, calculate expected completion time for casual and hardcore players, and set the premium price. The class evaluates each pass for value perception, pacing, and monetization balance.
setup_requirements: Battle pass design spreadsheet template
Feature flag service documentation (LaunchDarkly or similar)
Content calendar template for seasonal planning
LiveOps metrics dashboard examples

review.value: Players schedule their engagement around seasonal launches. A consistent 10-week season cadence with published start/end dates builds habitual re-engagement that sporadic updates cannot match.
A casual player (3 hours/week) should complete 60-70% of the free track. A dedicated player (10+ hours/week) should complete the premium track with 1-2 weeks to spare. Adjust XP curves accordingly.
The ability to enable, disable, or modify features server-side without a client update is the most important technical capability for live operations. Build this infrastructure early.
Content must be pre-produced 2-3 seasons ahead. A team producing content for the current season is already behind. Plan pipeline capacity based on delivery cadence, not crunch.

learning_objectives: Design server infrastructure that scales with player demand using cloud services
Implement auto-scaling policies that match server capacity to real-time player load
Plan for peak load scenarios including game launches, seasonal events, and viral growth
Calculate server costs and optimize infrastructure spending for different player populations
readings: AWS GameLift documentation and architecture guides
GDC Vault: 'Scaling Game Servers to a Million Players'
Case study: how Among Us scaled from 50 to 500,000 concurrent players

lecture_notes.introduction: A multiplayer game's server infrastructure must handle wildly variable load. On a normal Tuesday, your game might have 5,000 concurrent players. On launch day, a seasonal update, or after a viral moment, that number might spike to 500,000 in hours. If your infrastructure cannot scale to meet demand, players experience lag, disconnections, and failed logins, which is the fastest way to kill momentum for a live game. Conversely, over-provisioning servers for peak load wastes money during off-peak hours. Cloud infrastructure solves this through auto-scaling: dynamically adding and removing server instances based on real-time demand. This session covers the practical engineering of multiplayer server infrastructure: how to architect game servers for cloud deployment, how to implement auto-scaling policies, how to plan for peak load scenarios, and how to optimize costs. We will examine the major cloud gaming services (AWS GameLift, Google Cloud, Azure PlayFab) and analyze real-world scaling case studies.
key_concepts: Server architecture for cloud: containerized game servers (Docker), stateless server design where possible, shared state through external databases or caches (Redis)

Auto-scaling fundamentals: monitoring concurrent player count and server utilization, adding instances when load exceeds thresholds, removing instances when load drops, with appropriate warm-up and cool-down periods
AWS GameLift: managed game server hosting with matchmaking integration, auto-scaling, and multi-region deployment. Handles fleet management so developers focus on game logic.
Server fleet management: distributing servers across regions (US East, US West, EU, Asia), routing players to the nearest available server, and handling cross-region play
Instance type selection: CPU-intensive games need compute-optimized instances (c5), memory-intensive games need memory-optimized instances (r5). Right-sizing reduces costs.
Persistent vs. session-based server models: session servers spin up for a match and terminate after. Persistent servers (MMO world servers) run continuously and require different scaling strategies.
Database considerations: player profiles, inventories, and progression stored in databases (PostgreSQL, DynamoDB) with read replicas and caching layers to handle load
Peak load planning: launch day capacity (3-5x normal estimates), seasonal event capacity (2-3x normal), and viral spike capacity (10x+ with rapid scale-up). Always have a scaling plan.
Cost optimization: reserved instances for baseline load, spot/preemptible instances for variable load, and aggressive auto-scaling down during off-peak hours
Monitoring and alerting: real-time dashboards for concurrent players, server utilization, match queue times, error rates, and latency. Alert on anomalies before they become outages.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cloud deployment exercise: students deploy a simple multiplayer game server to AWS using GameLift or a containerized approach. They configure auto-scaling policies, simulate load by connecting multiple bot clients, and observe the infrastructure scaling up in response. They then examine the cost dashboard to understand the per-player-hour economics of cloud server hosting.
setup_requirements: AWS free tier account or provided cloud sandbox
Docker for containerizing game servers
Load testing tool or bot client for simulating player connections
Cloud cost calculator spreadsheet

review.value: Plan infrastructure that can handle 10x your expected concurrent players. Viral moments, streamer coverage, and free weekends can spike load by an order of magnitude with zero warning.

Cloud instances take 2-5 minutes to spin up. Scale up proactively based on trends, not reactively based on current load. A 5-minute gap between demand spike and new capacity causes mass disconnections.

A typical multiplayer game costs $0.001-0.01 per player-hour in server hosting. At 10,000 concurrent players, that is $240-2,400/day. Model costs before launch and ensure revenue supports infrastructure.
Deploy servers in at least 3 regions (NA, EU, Asia) for a global game. Route players to the nearest region by default. Cross-region play should be opt-in because of the latency penalty.

learning_objectives: Design moderation systems that scale from hundreds to millions of players
Implement automated content moderation using machine learning and rule-based systems
Create player reporting workflows with investigation, adjudication, and appeal processes
Balance free expression with community safety using clear policies and consistent enforcement
readings: Fair Play Alliance: 'Principles of Fair Play in Online Games'
Riot Games: 'How We Built the Tribunal (and What We Learned)'
GDC Vault: 'Moderating Online Communities at Scale'

lecture_notes.introduction: Community management at scale is one of the most challenging aspects of running a multiplayer game. A game with a million daily active players generates millions of chat messages, thousands of player reports, and hundreds of incidents requiring human review every single day. Without robust moderation tools and scalable processes, toxic behavior drives away the majority of players who want a positive experience. The challenge is balancing the need for safety and civility with the desire for free expression and community culture. Over-moderation kills community spirit; under-moderation allows toxicity to flourish. This session covers the tools and systems that enable effective community management at scale: automated moderation systems, player reporting workflows, punishment and rehabilitation systems, and the human processes that handle edge cases and appeals. We will examine how major multiplayer games approach moderation differently and what works at each scale.
key_concepts: Moderation at scale: human review does not scale. A game with 1M DAU generates far more content than any moderation team can review manually. Automation handles volume; humans handle nuance.

Automated text moderation: keyword filtering (basic but easily circumvented), ML-based toxicity scoring (Perspective API, custom models), and context-aware systems that distinguish banter from harassment
Automated voice moderation: real-time speech-to-text with toxicity scoring, triggered recording for reported incidents, and opt-in recording with player consent frameworks
Player reporting system: in-game report button, category selection (harassment, cheating, griefing, hate speech), optional description, and evidence collection (chat logs, replay snippets)
Investigation workflow: automated triage (high-confidence automated action, medium-confidence queued for human review, low-confidence logged for pattern detection), human review for escalated cases

Punishment framework: verbal warning, temporary mute, temporary ban, permanent ban. Each level with clear criteria, automated where possible, and a path to rehabilitation through behavior improvement.
Appeal process: players must have a way to contest punishments. Appeals reviewed by different moderator than original decision. False positive rate must be tracked and minimized.
Proactive vs. reactive moderation: reactive moderation responds to reports. Proactive moderation uses behavioral patterns to identify toxic players before they are reported.

Community guidelines: clear, specific rules published in-game and on the website. Players must acknowledge guidelines before accessing social features. Guidelines are the basis for all moderation decisions.
Moderator well-being: content moderation staff are exposed to the worst of human behavior. Rotation, mental health support, and exposure limits are essential for sustainable moderation operations.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Moderation system design exercise: students design a complete moderation pipeline for a multiplayer game with 100,000 DAU. They spec the automated detection layer, the reporting UI, the investigation workflow, the punishment tiers, and the appeal process. The class then role-plays moderation scenarios: obvious toxicity, ambiguous banter, false reports, and coordinated report abuse, evaluating how each system handles each case.
setup_requirements: Google Perspective API for toxicity scoring demonstration
Moderation workflow diagram template
Scenario cards for role-play exercises (provided on LMS)
Community guidelines template

review.value: Automated systems handle volume (95%+ of moderation actions). Humans handle edge cases, appeals, and policy decisions. Neither alone is sufficient at scale.

Players who exhibit toxic behavior in 3+ matches within 24 hours should be flagged for automatic intervention before reports arrive. Pattern detection catches serial offenders faster than reactive reporting.
Incorrectly punishing an innocent player is more damaging than missing a toxic player. Automated systems should err toward caution and queue uncertain cases for human review.
Limit content moderation shifts to 4 hours, provide mental health resources, rotate between moderation and other tasks, and never require solo review of the most severe content.

learning_objectives: Present a multiplayer game prototype demonstrating mastery of networking, social systems, and live operations design
Defend technical architecture decisions with performance data and trade-off analysis
Evaluate peer projects for networking robustness, game design quality, and scalability potential
Synthesize all course concepts into a cohesive multiplayer game design
readings: Review all course materials and project documentation
Prepare final project presentation with live demonstration
Peer evaluation rubric (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session showcases multiplayer game prototypes that demonstrate mastery of the concepts covered throughout the course. Each team presents a playable multiplayer game with networking, social features, and a design document covering matchmaking, anti-cheat, and live operations strategy. The prototype does not need to be a complete game, but it must demonstrate functional networking with at least two of the following: client-server authoritative networking with latency compensation, a matchmaking system, social features (friends, party, or chat), and a live operations plan with feature flags. The presentation includes a live multiplayer demonstration, a technical architecture overview, and a design document walkthrough. Peers evaluate each project using a professional rubric, and the evaluation panel will stress-test networking claims by introducing latency and packet loss during the live demonstration.
key_concepts: Project integration: combining networking architecture, game design, social systems, and operational infrastructure into a cohesive multiplayer experience
Technical demonstration: showing the game working under real network conditions, not just on localhost. Latency injection during the demo reveals the quality of networking implementation.
Architecture documentation: network topology diagram, tick rate justification, bandwidth measurements, and latency compensation approach with performance metrics
Design document components: game design overview, multiplayer-specific design decisions, matchmaking strategy, anti-cheat approach, social features plan, and live operations roadmap

Peer evaluation: assessing projects for networking robustness (does it handle latency gracefully?), game design quality (is the multiplayer experience compelling?), and scalability (could this architecture handle 1,000 players?)
Post-mortem reflection: what worked, what did not, what would you do differently with more time, and what you learned about multiplayer development that surprised you
Portfolio preparation: this project becomes a portfolio piece demonstrating multiplayer development capability to potential employers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their project in a 15-minute slot: 5 minutes live demo (including with injected latency), 5 minutes technical architecture walkthrough, 5 minutes Q&A. The instructor introduces network stress (200ms latency, 5% packet loss) during the demo to test robustness. Following presentations, the class conducts a structured retrospective discussing common challenges and lessons learned.
setup_requirements: Presentation hardware with network simulation capability
Two or more devices for live multiplayer demonstration
Network stress testing tools (Clumsy or NetEm)
Peer evaluation forms and presentation timer

review.value: A multiplayer game that only works on localhost is not a multiplayer game. The demo with injected latency reveals whether latency compensation is implemented or merely documented.
Every technical decision (client-server vs. P2P, tick rate, transport protocol) should be justified with specific reasoning related to the game's genre, player count, and latency requirements.
The design document should cover not just what was built but what would be needed for production: matchmaking, anti-cheat, moderation, scaling, and live ops. This demonstrates senior-level thinking.
A functional multiplayer prototype with networking, social features, and a comprehensive design document is one of the strongest portfolio pieces a game developer can have.

questions.question: You are designing a competitive 5v5 tactical shooter. Compare client-server and peer-to-peer architectures for this specific game. For each, explain the implications for cheat prevention, latency, server cost, and scalability. Conclude with your recommended architecture and justify your choice.
questions.answer: Client-server: Server is authoritative, preventing most cheats (speed hacks, teleportation, damage modification). Latency adds one hop (client to server to client), approximately doubling round-trip time compared to direct P2P. Server cost is significant: dedicated servers for 10 players per match. Scales well: add more servers for more concurrent matches. P2P: No server cost, lower latency between nearby peers. However, cheating is extremely difficult to prevent because each client has full game state access. Scalability is acceptable for 10 players (45 connection pairs). Recommendation: Client-server authoritative with dedicated servers. A competitive tactical shooter absolutely requires cheat prevention, and the latency cost is acceptable with proper lag compensation. The server cost is justified by maintaining competitive integrity.

rubric.key: Accurate comparison across all four criteria for both architectures
Cheat prevention correctly identified as the decisive factor for competitive games
Server cost implications correctly assessed for 5v5 match structure
Recommendation is justified with genre-specific reasoning

questions.question: Explain the server reconciliation process step by step. A client sends movement input at tick 100. The server processes it and responds at tick 103. The client has continued predicting to tick 106. Describe exactly what happens on the client when the server response arrives, including how unacknowledged inputs are handled.
questions.answer: Step 1: Client receives server authoritative state for tick 100 at client tick 106. Step 2: Client compares its predicted state at tick 100 against the server's authoritative state at tick 100. Step 3: If they match, no correction needed (prediction was accurate). Step 4: If they differ, the client resets its state to the server's authoritative state at tick 100. Step 5: The client has inputs buffered for ticks 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, and 106 (unacknowledged inputs). Step 6: The client replays all unacknowledged inputs (101-106) against the server's authoritative tick 100 state, producing a new predicted state at tick 106. Step 7: The client renders this corrected state. If the correction is small, the player does not notice. If large, the player may see a brief position snap.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the comparison between predicted and authoritative state
Accurately describes input buffer and replay of unacknowledged inputs
Explains the visual impact when correction is small vs. large

questions.question: A fighting game developer is choosing between delay-based and rollback netcode. The game runs at 60fps and targets a global audience with latencies up to 150ms one-way. Calculate the input delay in frames for delay-based netcode at 150ms. Then explain why rollback is preferred and what technical requirement it imposes on the game engine.
questions.answer: At 60fps, one frame is 16.67ms. Delay-based netcode adds delay equal to one-way latency: 150ms / 16.67ms = 9 frames of input delay. This is unplayable for a fighting game where single-frame reactions matter. Rollback eliminates perceived input delay for the local player by predicting the remote player's inputs and simulating forward immediately. When predictions are wrong (which is rare for frame-to-frame input continuity), the game rolls back to the last confirmed state and re-simulates. The critical technical requirement is deterministic simulation: the game must produce bit-identical results from the same inputs on every machine. This means no floating-point non-determinism, no random number generators without synchronized seeds, and state must be serializable and restorable rapidly every frame.

rubric.key: Correct frame delay calculation (9 frames at 150ms/60fps)
Clear explanation of why rollback eliminates perceived delay
Determinism requirement explained with specific technical constraints

questions.question: In a client-server FPS, Player A (50ms ping) shoots at Player B (200ms ping) who just ran behind a wall. From Player A's perspective, the shot hit. From Player B's perspective, they were already behind cover. The server uses lag compensation. Explain exactly how the server resolves this situation, whose perspective wins, and why this is the correct behavior despite feeling unfair to Player B.
questions.answer: The server receives Player A's shot command tagged with Player A's client timestamp. The server rolls back the game state to Player A's timestamp minus Player A's half-RTT (25ms ago from the server's current time). At that rewound point in time, the server checks Player B's position from Player A's perspective. Player B was still visible (not yet behind the wall from Player A's viewpoint). The shot hits. Player A's perspective wins because lag compensation prioritizes the shooter's experience. The alternative (server checks current positions) would make all shots miss at high latency, making the game unplayable for anyone with non-zero ping. The trade-off is that Player B experiences being hit 'behind cover,' which is a consequence of their own latency. The server resolved correctly: at the moment Player A pulled the trigger, the shot was valid. This is the universally accepted trade-off because the alternative (no lag compensation) makes shooting feel broken for everyone.

rubric.key: Correctly describes server time-rewind process using shooter's timestamp
Explains why the shooter's perspective is used and the alternative's downside
Acknowledges the trade-off for the victim with honest assessment

questions.question: Your multiplayer game with 32 players has a bandwidth budget of 128 kbps per player. Each player entity has: position (3 floats, 12 bytes), rotation (1 quaternion, 16 bytes), velocity (3 floats, 12 bytes), health (1 int, 4 bytes), and animation state (1 int, 4 bytes). At 30 Hz tick rate, calculate the naive bandwidth requirement. Then describe three specific optimization techniques and estimate the bandwidth after optimization.
questions.answer: Naive calculation: Per entity: 12 + 16 + 12 + 4 + 4 = 48 bytes. For 32 players (each client sees 31 others): 31 * 48 = 1,488 bytes per tick. At 30 Hz: 1,488 * 30 = 44,640 bytes/second = 357 kbps. This exceeds the 128 kbps budget by nearly 3x. Optimization 1: Delta compression. Only 10-20% of fields change each tick (players standing still, constant health). Average delta: ~10 bytes per entity. New: 31 * 10 * 30 = 9,300 B/s = 74 kbps. Optimization 2: Quantization.
