Position quantized to 2 bytes per axis (6 bytes vs. 12). Rotation to 3 bytes (smallest-three encoding). Saves ~50% on the data that does change. New: ~37 kbps. Optimization 3: Interest management. Players only receive updates for the 15 nearest entities instead of all 31. Halves the entity count. New: ~18.5 kbps. Post-optimization: approximately 18-20 kbps, well within the 128 kbps budget with room for game events, chat, and overhead.

rubric.key: Correct naive bandwidth calculation with shown work
Delta compression explained with realistic reduction estimate
Quantization techniques described with bit/byte savings
Interest management reduces entity count with reasonable threshold

questions.question: Explain entity interpolation and why remote players are rendered 'in the past.' What happens if a snapshot is lost due to packet loss? How does the system handle jitter (variable latency)?
questions.answer: Entity interpolation renders remote players at a position between two received server snapshots, typically 100ms behind real-time. The client buffers snapshots and smoothly interpolates between them rather than snapping to each new position. This adds visual latency but produces smooth, jitter-free movement. If a snapshot is lost (UDP is unreliable): the client still has the previous snapshot and can continue interpolating toward the next received snapshot, or fall back to extrapolation (predicting forward from the last known position and velocity). Extrapolation is less accurate but prevents visual freezing. Jitter handling: the interpolation buffer size adapts dynamically. During stable conditions, the buffer is tight (2-3 snapshots, ~100ms). During jitter (variable snapshot arrival times), the buffer expands (4-5 snapshots, ~150-200ms) to absorb timing variance. This adds slightly more visual latency but prevents stuttering. The buffer contracts again when conditions stabilize.

rubric.key: Correctly explains interpolation between buffered snapshots with latency trade-off
Describes fallback behavior when snapshots are lost
Adaptive buffer explanation for jitter handling

questions.question: Design a matchmaking system for a 5v5 competitive game with 50,000 concurrent players. Specify the rating system, queue parameters, party handling rules, and new player calibration. Address what happens during off-peak hours when only 5,000 players are online.
questions.answer: Rating system: TrueSkill or Glicko-2 (better for team games than ELO). Each player has a skill mean (mu) and uncertainty (sigma). Queue parameters: initial matching within +/- 1 sigma, expanding by 0.5 sigma every 30 seconds up to a maximum wait of 3 minutes. Target ping under 60ms, expanding to 100ms after 90 seconds. Party handling: parties of 2-3 are matched against other parties when possible. Maximum 1 division rank difference between party members in ranked. Party MMR weighted toward the highest-ranked member (80/20 weight). New player calibration: 10 placement matches with 3x normal rating adjustment. Start at median rating with high sigma (high uncertainty). Match against diverse opponents to quickly converge. Off-peak (5,000 players): relax skill matching by 50% (wider acceptable range), expand regional boundaries to include adjacent regions, and display estimated queue time to set expectations. Below 2,000 concurrent, offer AI backfill with disclosure.

rubric.key: Appropriate rating system selection with justification for team games
Queue parameters include expanding search with specific thresholds
Party handling addresses rank restrictions and MMR weighting
Off-peak degradation strategy is practical and honest with players

questions.question: Explain three layers of anti-cheat defense and provide a specific example of a cheat that each layer catches. For each layer, identify a cheat that it cannot detect and explain why.
questions.answer: Layer 1: Server-side validation. Catches: speed hacks (server validates that player position change per tick does not exceed max movement speed). Cannot detect: wallhacks (the server must send nearby enemy positions to the client for rendering; the client can display them through walls without modifying any server-validated value). Layer 2: Client-side anti-cheat software (EAC, BattlEye). Catches: known aimbot software detected through signature scanning of injected DLLs, modified game memory, and hooked functions. Cannot detect: private cheats with unique signatures that have not been catalogued, or cheats that operate at the hardware level (external aim devices that read screen pixels). Layer 3: Statistical behavioral analysis. Catches: aimbots that produce inhuman accuracy patterns (reaction times consistently under 150ms, headshot percentage above 80%, perfect tracking through direction changes). Cannot detect: soft aimbots that subtly assist aim within human-plausible parameters, or cheaters who deliberately underperform periodically to mask their statistics.

rubric.key: Three distinct layers correctly described with specific cheat examples caught
Each layer's blind spot correctly identified with technical explanation
Understanding that no single layer is sufficient demonstrated

questions.question: A player reports that your cooperative PvE game 'doesn't feel like a team game' because one high-level player always carries the group. Propose three specific cooperative design changes that would create genuine interdependence and prevent carrying. For each, explain the mechanism and potential downsides.
questions.answer: Change 1: Simultaneous activation puzzles. Encounters require two or more players to activate switches, stand on pressure plates, or channel abilities simultaneously. The carry player cannot be in two places at once. Mechanism: environmental mechanics that hard-require multiple actors. Downside: frustrating with disconnected players or poor coordination; requires fallback for smaller groups. Change 2: Class-exclusive traversal and interaction. Only certain classes can access certain areas or interact with certain objects (engineer hacks terminals, scout reaches high ledges). Key objectives require multiple class interactions. Mechanism: the carry player can only fill one role regardless of skill. Downside: locks team composition, reduces flexibility, can feel restrictive. Change 3: Contribution-scaled rewards with performance floor. Each player must meet a minimum contribution threshold (damage dealt, objectives completed, revives performed) to receive full rewards. Below the floor, rewards are reduced. Mechanism: creates individual accountability within the cooperative framework. Downside: can pressure underperforming players negatively; must be tuned so the floor is achievable by all skill levels.

rubric.key: Three distinct mechanisms that genuinely prevent single-player carry
Each mechanism's implementation is specific enough to be actionable
Honest downside analysis for each proposal

questions.question: Your competitive game's data shows the following: Character A has a 58% win rate with 35% pick rate. Character B has a 45% win rate with 8% pick rate. Character C has a 52% win rate with 20% pick rate. Analyze the balance state of each character, recommend specific balance changes, and explain the risk of each change.
questions.answer: Character A: Overpowered. 58% win rate is significantly above 50% baseline, and the high 35% pick rate means this is not a niche counter-pick but a generalist powerhouse. Recommendation: nerf A's strongest ability by 10-15% or increase its cooldown. Risk: over-nerfing could drop A to unviable, and the large player base invested in A will be vocal. Apply moderate nerfs across multiple patches rather than one large nerf. Character B: Underpowered. 45% win rate with only 8% pick rate means dedicated mains are performing poorly and the general population avoids the character. Recommendation: buff B's core ability by 10-15% and improve quality-of-life issues. Risk: buff might not be enough to shift perception (players have already given up on B). Consider a rework if buffs do not improve pick rate within two patches. Character C: Balanced. 52% win rate is within the healthy 48-52% range, and 20% pick rate shows solid popularity without dominance. Recommendation: no changes needed. Risk: changing C to address other characters' viability could inadvertently destabilize C. Protect balanced characters from collateral changes.

rubric.key: Correct identification of A as overpowered using both win rate and pick rate
B correctly identified as underpowered with pick rate as supporting evidence
C correctly identified as balanced with specific healthy range cited
Balance change recommendations include specific magnitudes and risk assessment

questions.question: Design a battle pass for a 10-week season of a competitive multiplayer game. Specify the number of tiers, the pacing curve, free vs. premium tier split, and how a casual player (3 hours/week) and a hardcore player (15 hours/week) experience progression. Address FOMO concerns.
questions.answer: Structure: 100 tiers (70 free, 30 premium-exclusive). Premium price: $10. Pacing: first 30 tiers require 50% less XP each (quick early gratification). Tiers 30-70 require standard XP. Tiers 70-100 require 25% more XP (stretch goals for dedicated players). XP sources: daily challenges (3 per day, 1,500 XP each), weekly challenges (3 per week, 5,000 XP each), and passive play XP (100 XP per match). Casual player (3 hrs/week): completes ~2 daily challenges and all weeklies. Earns approximately 40,000 XP/week. Over 10 weeks: reaches tier 60-65 of the free track. Gets meaningful rewards but misses the final tiers. Hardcore player (15 hrs/week): completes all dailies and weeklies plus significant passive XP. Earns approximately 90,000 XP/week. Reaches tier 100 by week 7-8 with time to spare. FOMO mitigation: end-of-season 'catch-up' week with doubled XP, ability to purchase remaining tiers at 150 in-game currency each (earnable without spending money), and a rotating 'legacy pass' that allows completing previous season passes for a fee.

rubric.key: Tier structure with clear free/premium split and pricing
Pacing curve is front-loaded for early gratification with shown calculations
Casual and hardcore player experiences calculated with realistic numbers
FOMO mitigation strategies are concrete and player-friendly

questions.question: Explain three specific strategies for scaling multiplayer server infrastructure during a surprise viral moment that causes a 20x spike in concurrent players over 4 hours. For each strategy, explain the implementation and the time it takes to take effect.
questions.answer: Strategy 1: Cloud auto-scaling with aggressive policies. Pre-configured auto-scaling rules that add server instances when average CPU exceeds 60% or queue times exceed 60 seconds. Implementation: configured in advance through AWS GameLift fleet policies or Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaler. Time to effect: 3-5 minutes for new instances to spin up and register as available. This is the first line of defense. Strategy 2: Multi-region overflow. When the primary region reaches capacity limits, route new connections to adjacent regions with available capacity. US-East overflow to US-West, EU overflow to US-East with higher latency accepted. Implementation: global load balancer with region health checks and overflow routing rules. Time to effect: immediate once configured, but requires pre-positioned capacity in overflow regions. Strategy 3: Graceful degradation. Reduce server-side processing to handle more players per instance: lower tick rate from 64 Hz to 30 Hz, disable non-essential features (cosmetic effects, replay recording, detailed analytics), and queue players for matchmaking rather than rejecting connections. Implementation: feature flags that toggle degraded mode when load exceeds thresholds. Time to effect: immediate (server-side configuration change), but player experience is reduced.

rubric.key: Auto-scaling correctly described with realistic spin-up time
Multi-region overflow with latency trade-off acknowledged
Graceful degradation with specific features to disable prioritized
Time-to-effect estimates are realistic for each strategy

questions.question: Your multiplayer game has been review-bombed on Steam following a controversial balance patch. The review score dropped from 82% to 61% positive in 48 hours. 60% of the negative reviews specifically mention the nerf to a popular character. Outline a response strategy covering community communication, review management, and balance decision-making.
questions.answer: Immediate (within 24 hours): Post an official community statement acknowledging the feedback. Be specific: 'We hear your concerns about the [Character] changes.' Do not be defensive. Explain the balance data that motivated the change (win rate, pick rate). Commit to monitoring the impact over the next 1-2 weeks with specific metrics. Do not promise to revert. Community communication: Host a live Q&A or post a detailed developer blog explaining the balance philosophy, the data behind the decision, and the team's approach to future adjustments. Transparency about methodology reduces perception that changes are arbitrary. Review management: Do not respond to individual negative reviews aggressively. Pin a developer update in the Steam community hub linking to the detailed explanation. Steam allows developers to respond to reviews; use this to point to the explanation post, not to argue. Balance decision-making: Analyze post-patch data after 1-2 weeks (the meta needs time to stabilize). If the character's win rate dropped below 47% and pick rate below 5%, partially revert the nerf (restore 50% of the reduction). Communicate this as 'data-driven adjustment' not 'caving to pressure.' Long-term: Implement a Public Test Realm for balance changes so the community can provide feedback before patches go live.

rubric.key: Immediate acknowledgment without being defensive or promising reversion
Transparent communication sharing the balance data that motivated the change
Data-driven response plan with specific metrics and timeline for evaluation
Long-term process improvement (PTR, community feedback integration)

questions.question: Design a moderation system for a multiplayer game with 200,000 daily active players. The game has text chat, voice chat, and gameplay reporting. Specify the automated layer, human review layer, punishment tiers, and appeal process. Include realistic staffing estimates.
questions.answer: Automated layer: Text chat runs through ML toxicity classifier (Perspective API or custom model) in real-time. Messages scoring above 0.9 confidence are automatically censored. Messages scoring 0.7-0.9 are flagged for review. Voice chat: opt-in recording when a report is filed. Automated speech-to-text with toxicity scoring on reported clips. Gameplay reporting: automated detection of intentional feeding (0 damage, standing still), AFKing (no input for 2+ minutes), and impossible statistics (cheat detection). Human review layer: Staff reviews flagged content (0.7-0.9 confidence), escalated reports, and appeal cases. Estimated volume: with 200K DAU generating approximately 1,000 reports/day, and automated systems handling 80%, human reviewers process ~200 cases/day. At 20-25 cases per reviewer per shift, need 8-10 moderators. Punishment tiers: Tier 1 (first offense): automated warning + 2-hour text mute. Tier 2 (second offense within 30 days): 24-hour suspension from competitive modes. Tier 3 (third offense): 7-day full suspension. Tier 4 (fourth offense or severe violation): permanent ban pending human review. Appeal process: submit appeal through support ticket with 48-hour response SLA. Different reviewer than original decision. False positive tracking: if appeal rate exceeds 20% for a detection type, retrain the model.

rubric.key: Automated detection with confidence thresholds for text, voice, and gameplay
Realistic staffing estimates based on report volume and processing capacity
Graduated punishment tiers with specific durations and escalation
Appeal process with SLA, independent review, and false positive monitoring

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'feature flags' in live game operations. Provide three specific scenarios where feature flags would be used in a multiplayer game and describe what would happen without them in each scenario.
questions.answer: Feature flags are server-side toggles that enable or disable features without requiring a client update. They allow real-time control over game behavior from a management dashboard. Scenario 1: Emergency kill switch. A new cosmetic item causes a rendering crash for 10% of players. With feature flag: disable the item server-side immediately, all clients stop rendering it. Without: must build, test, certify, and deploy a client patch, taking hours to days while players crash. Scenario 2: Gradual rollout. A new matchmaking algorithm is ready for testing. With feature flag: enable for 5% of players, monitor match quality metrics, gradually increase to 100% if metrics are positive. Without: deploy to everyone at once, risk degrading the experience for the entire player base if the algorithm has an issue. Scenario 3: Time-limited event. A holiday event runs for 2 weeks with special game modes and cosmetics. With feature flag: schedule activation and deactivation dates server-side, event starts and ends exactly on time. Without: release a client patch to add the event and another to remove it, with console certification adding days to the timeline on both ends.

rubric.key: Clear definition of feature flags as server-side toggles without client updates
Three distinct and realistic scenarios with correct feature flag usage
Without-flag consequences are accurate and demonstrate the stakes

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions, especially lab sessions where multiplayer testing requires multiple participants. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Multiplayer prototypes must be submitted with a working build; source code alone is insufficient. Lab partners may submit joint reports with individual contribution statements.
course_policies.collaboration: Team projects (up to 3 members) are encouraged for the final project. All team members must demonstrate individual understanding during Q&A. Code sharing between teams for networking infrastructure is permitted with attribution; game design must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All networking code must be original or properly attributed. Using Unity Netcode, Photon, or Mirror as frameworks is expected; presenting framework features as custom work is dishonest. AI assistance must be disclosed.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Unity 2023.2+ with Netcode for GameObjects, Wireshark, Python 3 for simulations, network simulation tools (Clumsy on Windows or NetEm on Linux), and a reliable local network or two machines for testing.
